segments.text: Woman: Good morning.
Woman: How may I help you?
Man: Good morning.
Man: We spoke on the phone last week, and I'm ringing to get more details about the job.
Woman: That's right.
Woman: Thank you for sending in your CV.
Woman: We've carefully looked at it, and I think we have several jobs that might suit you.
Man: That's great!
Woman: The first one is in a magazine based in North London.
Woman: They're looking for a reporter.
Man: What sort of magazine is it?
Woman: They're called Home Solutions, which publishes articles on housing every week, and they want to find someone responsible for collecting the latest news and writing relevant articles.
Man: What are the hours of work?
Woman: Well, you will be expected to work forty hours a week, but there may be opportunities to do overtime during busy periods.
Woman: For example, some may work more than fifty hours a week and get extra pay.
Woman: But you're not required to do this.
Man: That's reasonable.
Man: Do you know what the rates of pay are?
Woman: Yes.
Woman: It says you'll get a monthly pay of 7000 pounds for the first six months.
Woman: And after the probationary period, you'll be eligible for a salary of more than that, maybe 7500 pounds or more.
Man: That's good.
Woman: So, is that something that might interest you?
Man: It is, yes.
Man: The only thing is, you say they are in North London, so it would be a pretty long commute for me.
Woman: In that case, the second position might suit you better regarding location.
Woman: That's a researcher for an electronic engineering company in South London.
Man: Yes, that would be a lot closer.
Woman: Your CV shows you have relevant working experience in this field.
Man: Yes, I worked in the office of a computer engineering company for two years.
Man: What about the working hours?
Woman: For this job, you'll have to work 45 hours a week and the starting salary is 7200 pounds for the six-month probationary period.
Man: That should be nice.
Woman: I can arrange for you an interview if you're interested.
Woman: But before that, may I ask where you heard about us?
Woman: Was it in an advert or did your friend tell you?
Man: Neither.
Man: It was a website.
Man: Then I just got your number from the phone book.
Woman: Oh, right.
Woman: Apart from full-time jobs, we have some part-time work that might suit you.
Woman: Here several magazines are recruiting part-time writers.
Woman: Are you interested?
Man: What kind of writer exactly?
Man: I mean, what types of articles should I write about?
Woman: Well, the first one is to do with sports reports.
Woman: For this, you're expected to write sports articles twice weekly.
Man: Well, I love sports.
Man: I'm a football fan.
Man: But as far as writing is concerned, I'm not entirely keen on that.
Man: I think I'll rule it out.
Woman: OK.
Woman: Then how about writing something for entertainment?
Woman: Does it appeal to you?
Man: Entertainment is an exciting topic.
Man: I like reading news or articles in this category, but I'm not sure if I should write something about it.
Man: Anyway, I'll think about that.
Man: Could you give me more details?
Woman: OK, I've got your email, and I will send the details to you later.
Man: Thanks.
Woman: And the third one is writing reviews for soap dramas.
Man: Oh, soap drama is my favourite.
Man: I worked as a freelance writer in this field three years ago.
Man: I think it's pretty worthwhile to give my own opinions and recommendations to the audience.
Woman: That's good.
Woman: And the last one is writing articles for a business economy magazine.
Man: I'm unfamiliar with this field, so I think it might not be easy.
Woman: Don't worry too much.
Woman: Experience is not essential for this job.
Woman: They've got a training session for every recruit.
Man: That sounds not too bad.
Woman: OK, would you like me to send you the pamphlet so that you can learn more about it?
Man: That will be nice.
Woman: OK, that's it.
Woman: I'll send you all the information later and ...
